findings.title: No code/data for the visual-reasoning pass@k results (Fig. 4 right)
findings.quote: We use the EasyR1 framework [31] to train Qwen2.5-VL7B [32] on Geometry3K [33], and evaluate its visual reasoning capabilities on filtered MathVistaTestMini [34] and MathVision-TestMini [35]
findings.claim: The repo ships only math (`math/`) and DeepCoder code-RL training (`code/DeepCoder/`); a recursive grep for mathvista/mathvision/easyr1/geometry3k/qwen2.5-vl finds no eval, data, or training code for the visual-reasoning experiments.
findings.concern: The visual-reasoning pass@k curves (Fig. 4 right), a third of the paper's cross-modality evidence, cannot be reproduced or traced to any artefact in the repo.
findings.resolution: Authors: release (or link) the EasyR1 multimodal training config, the MathVista/MathVision filtered eval data, and the pass@k computation for the visual setting.
findings.reason: Grep across the full repo (*.py, *.sh, *.md, *.txt) finds no mathvista/mathvision/easyr1/geometry3k/qwen2.5-vl code except a README link to the external EasyR1 repo; paper quote confirmed at paper_text.txt line 544.

findings.title: No eval harness for code-generation pass@k (HumanEval+/MBPP+, Fig. 4 left)
findings.quote: Figure 4: Pass@k curves of base and RLVR models. (Left) Code Generation. (Right) Visual Reasoning.
findings.claim: The repo's `code/` tree contains only the DeepCoder/verl training framework; there is no HumanEval+/MBPP+ evaluation harness that produces the Fig. 4 (left) pass@k curves for Coder-R1-Zero. The README only credits Code-R1 (external) for code eval.
findings.concern: The code-generation pass@k results cannot be reproduced from this repo; the per-sample correctness used for pass@k on HumanEval+/MBPP+ is computed by an external/unshipped harness.
findings.resolution: Authors: include or link the exact HumanEval+/MBPP+ generation + unit-test scoring + pass@k script used for Fig. 4 (left).
findings.reason: The only HumanEval+ reference in the repo is in DeepCoder's training-data processing script (deepcoder_dataset.py:91), not an evaluation harness; no pass@k eval for HumanEval+/MBPP+ exists anywhere in the repo; Fig. 4 caption confirmed at paper_text.txt line 602.

findings.title: No script computes the base-vs-RL solvable-set overlap (Tables 2, 5, 6)
findings.quote:
Table 2: We evaluate on AIME24 (k = 1024)
and MATH500 (k = 128). The table reports the
solvable/unsolvable fraction of problems falling
into four categories.
findings.claim: `evaluate.py`/`pass@k.py` produce per-problem score matrices, but no script in the repo intersects the base-model and RL-model solvable sets to produce the four-cell coverage fractions of Table 2 (e.g. 63.3%/13.3%/0.0%/23.3% on AIME24) or the 'RL-solved is nearly a subset of base-solved' Tables 5/6.
findings.concern: The set-overlap statistics that underpin the paper's core 'reasoning paths already present in the base model' conclusion are not traceable to any computing artefact.
findings.resolution: Authors: provide the script that, given the two saved score jsonls, computes the four-cell coverage table and the subset/overlap statistics.
findings.reason: Grep for overlap/intersect/solvable/coverage/four-cell in evaluate.py and pass@k.py returns empty; Table 2 quote confirmed at paper_text.txt lines 650-653; no set-intersection script exists anywhere in the repo.

findings.title: No perplexity-computation code for the §4.1 perplexity analysis
findings.quote:
PPLm(Y | x) = exp
 
−1
T
T
X
t=1
log P (yt | x, y1, . . . , yt−1)
findings.claim: The paper's perplexity analysis (a load-bearing argument that RL-model responses lie within the base model's distribution) requires per-token log-likelihood scoring; a recursive grep for perplexity/log_likelihood/logprob over `math/` and `code/scripts` finds no such computation.
findings.concern: The perplexity-based evidence for the 'reasoning paths already present in base models' claim cannot be reproduced from the repo.
findings.resolution: Authors: release the script that computes PPL_m(Y|x) for base/RL/distilled models on the response sets reported in §4.1.
cross_refs: §4.1 Reasoning Paths Already Present in Base Models
findings.reason: Grep for perplexity/log_likelihood/logprob/ppl in math/*.py and code/scripts finds no perplexity computation (the 'ppl' hits in math_eval.py are substring matches in 'apply_chat_template'); PPL formula confirmed in paper_text.txt at lines 689-696.

findings.title: Manual CoT-validity check (§3.1) has no code; relies on human inspection
findings.quote:
To address this, we
manually check the correctness of CoT for a subset of model outputs as detailed in Section 3.1.
findings.claim: The paper mitigates the 'lucky-guess' threat to pass@k by manually inspecting CoTs of the hardest problems; this is an off-code human procedure with no script, selection criterion file, or annotation artefact in the repo.
findings.concern: The reader cannot independently verify which outputs were inspected or how guess-only correct answers were excluded, so the guessing-correction step is unauditable.
findings.resolution: Authors: release the inspected-sample IDs and annotation outcomes (even as a CSV) so the CoT-validity claim can be checked.
findings.paper_ref: Section 3.1 Random Guessing Issue; Section D.2
findings.reason: Paper explicitly uses 'manually check' phrasing at paper_text.txt lines 398 and 1518; no annotation/CSV/selection-criterion artefact exists in the repo; the claim is factual and the severity is already low.

findings.title: Math-eval imports (wandb, pandas, matplotlib) not in requirements.txt
findings.quote:
accelerate
codetiming
datasets
dill
hydra-core==1.4.0.dev1
omegaconf==2.4.0.dev3
numpy
pybind11
ray[default]==2.10.0
tensordict
transformers<4.48
vllm<=0.6.3
peft
liger-kernel
word2number
math-verify[antlr4_11_0]==0.6.0
findings.claim: `collect_results.py` imports `pandas`, `matplotlib`, and `wandb` (unconditionally) and `eval.sh` runs `math_eval.py` which transitively needs `pebble`/`jieba`/`sympy`/`antlr4`; none of pandas/matplotlib/wandb appear in requirements.txt (pebble/jieba/sympy/antlr4 are installed ad hoc inside `math_eval.sh`).
findings.concern: A clean `pip install -e .` does not yield a runnable evaluation+aggregation environment; the result-collection step fails on import without manually adding the missing packages.
findings.resolution: Authors: add pandas, matplotlib, wandb (and the math_eval.sh-installed pebble/jieba/sympy/antlr4 pins) to requirements.txt, or document them.
findings.reason: requirements.txt (lines 1-16) confirmed to contain none of pandas/matplotlib/wandb; math/examples/math_eval/sh/collect_results.py lines 6-13 unconditionally import all three; requirements.txt quote matches exactly.

findings.title: vLLM max_model_len hardcoded to 4096, contradicting documented max_tokens=16000
findings.quote:
if args.use_vllm:
    llm = LLM(
        model=args.model_name_or_path,
        tensor_parallel_size=len(available_gpus) // args.pipeline_parallel_size,
        pipeline_parallel_size=args.pipeline_parallel_size,
        trust_remote_code=True,
        seed=args.seed,
        gpu_memory_utilization=0.9,
        max_model_len=4096,
    )
findings.claim: The vLLM engine's total context length (prompt + generation) is hardcoded to 4096, while the script accepts `--max_tokens 16000` (README/eval_math_nodes.sh) and forwards it as `SamplingParams(max_tokens=args.max_tokens_per_call)` at line 307; vLLM can only emit up to `4096 - prompt_len` new tokens regardless of the requested 16000.
findings.concern: Long reasoning chains on hard benchmarks (AIME/Olympiad at large k) are silently truncated to <4096 tokens instead of the documented 16000, which can suppress exactly the long-CoT correct samples that the large-k coverage comparison depends on.
findings.resolution: Authors: confirm the max_model_len used for the reported runs; if 4096 was actually used, reconcile it with the README's `--max_tokens 16000`, or expose max_model_len as a CLI argument tied to max_tokens.
findings.reason: max_model_len=4096 confirmed at math_eval.py:139; SamplingParams(max_tokens=args.max_tokens_per_call) confirmed at line 307; eval_math_nodes.sh line 24 sets max_tokens=16000; check_context_cap.py copied to _verifier_code/, ran successfully, and output confirms the conflict (_verifier_code/out/context_cap.txt).

findings.title: eval.sh comment claims aime24/amc23 use n_sampling=8, code uses the passed N_SAMPLING
findings.quote:
# Run special benchmarks (aime24, amc23) with n_sampling=8
if [ ${#SPECIAL_BENCHMARKS[@]} -gt 0 ]; then
    SPECIAL_BENCHMARKS_STR=$(IFS=,; echo "${SPECIAL_BENCHMARKS[*]}")
    TOKENIZERS_PARALLELISM=false \
    python -u math_eval.py \
        --model_name_or_path ${MODEL_NAME_OR_PATH} \
        --data_name ${SPECIAL_BENCHMARKS_STR} \
        --output_dir ${OUTPUT_DIR} \
        --split ${SPLIT} \
        --prompt_type ${PROMPT_TYPE} \
        --num_test_sample ${NUM_TEST_SAMPLE} \
        --max_tokens_per_call ${max_tokens} \
        --seed ${seed} \
        --temperature ${temperature} \
        --n_sampling ${N_SAMPLING} \
        --top_p ${top_p} \
findings.claim: The comment says special benchmarks (aime24/amc23) run with n_sampling=8, but the actual invocation passes `--n_sampling ${N_SAMPLING}`, i.e. whatever was given on the command line (32, 256, 512, …) — identical to the regular branch.
findings.concern: The stale comment could mislead a reproducer into thinking aime24/amc23 are forced to a different sampling count than other benchmarks; the code in fact does not differ on this axis (only the temperature=0 special-casing differs).
findings.resolution: Authors: delete or correct the `n_sampling=8` comment; confirm the per-benchmark n values (paper uses n=1024 for AIME24/AMC23) were set via the CLI.
findings.reason: eval.sh line 64 comment 'n_sampling=8' and line 78 '--n_sampling ${N_SAMPLING}' both confirmed at the cited lines; the mismatch between comment and code is real and unambiguous.
